session.user:
In the file `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/commands/erk/learn.md`:
This file documents a CLI command. The project has renamed programmatic field names from "plan" to "pr" in the API/CLI. Update this file to match.
**Rename these patterns:** 1. `plan_number` → `pr_number` (variable names, JSON field names) 2. `plan_url` → `pr_url` (variable names, JSON field names) 3. `plan_title` → `pr_title` (variable names, JSON field names) 4. `plan-number` → `pr-number` (kebab-case in argument hints, placeholders like `<plan-number>`) 5. `plan #<` or `plan #N` → `PR #<` or `PR #N` (when referring to the programmatic PR entity in output messages)
**DO NOT rename:** - "plan" as a concept noun (e.g., "implementation plan", "plan mode", "learn plan", "the plan") - `plan.md` file references - `--plan-type` flag - Skill/command names containing "plan" (e.g., `/erk:plan-save`) - `plan_backend` field name (this was NOT renamed) - `plan_content` references (not renamed) - "erk-plan" or "erk-learn" label names - The word "plan" in headings, descriptions, or conceptual text
Read the file first, then apply all renames using the Edit tool. Be careful to distinguish between programmatic field names (rename) and conceptual usage (keep).
session.assistant: I'll start by reading the file to understand its current structure and identify what needs to be renamed.
session.tool_result:
1→--- 2→description: Extract insights from plan-associated sessions 3→argument-hint: "[plan-number]" 4→--- 5→ 6→# /erk:learn 7→ 8→Create a documentation plan from Claude Code sessions associated with a plan implementation. The verb "learn" means: analyze what happened, extract insights, and create an actionable plan to document those learnings. 9→ 10→## Usage 11→ 12→``` 13→/erk:learn # Infers plan from current branch 14→/erk:learn 4655 # Explicit plan number 15→``` 16→ 17→## Purpose 18→ 19→All documentation produced by this command follows the content quality standards in the `learned-docs` skill. Load that skill for the full rules on audience (AI agents), the cornerstone principle (cross-cutting insight), and what belongs in learned docs vs code comments. 20→ 21→## Agent Instructions 22→ 23→**Prerequisite:** Load the `learned-docs` skill for content quality standards before proceeding. 24→ 25→Tell the user (choose the appropriate pipeline display based on whether `.erk/impl-context/` exists): 26→ 27→**When `.erk/impl-context/` exists (CI/async mode):** 28→ 29→``` 30→Learn pipeline for plan #<plan-number> (using preprocessed materials):
... [802 lines omitted] ...
55→Parse the JSON output and check the `labels` array. If `erk-learn` is present, stop with this error: 58→Error: Plan #<plan-number> is a learn plan (has erk-learn label). 143→This returns JSON with PR details (`number`, `title`, `state`, `url`, `head_ref_name`, `base_ref_name`) or an error if no PR exists. Save the PR number for the parallel agents below. 414→Confirm you see the expected files (session-\*.md, diff-analysis.md, existing-docs-check.md, pr-comments-analysis.md) before proceeding. If any files are missing, the agent failed to write its output and must be re-launched. 545→If agents were not launched or failed, fall back to manual analysis. 574→- Error messages and how they were resolved 743→Parse the JSON output. If `count` is 0, no comment was added (no candidates found by the extractor). This is normal and not an error. 759→**If plan validation failed (no actionable documentation):** 825→- `<tool_result>` elements with errors often reveal the most useful insights

Line 52: `<plan-number>` → `<pr-number>` 5. Line 58: `Plan #<plan-number>` → `PR #<pr-number>` 6. Line 105: `<plan-number>` → `<pr-number>` 7. Line 130: `plan #<plan-number>` → `PR #<pr-number>` 8. Line 140: `get-pr-for-plan <plan-number>` → `get-pr-for-plan <pr-number>` 9. Line 152: `<plan-number>` → `<pr-number>` 10. Line 254, 300-301: `<pr-number>` and `<plan-number>` usage 11. Line 327-328: `plan_title` → `pr_title` and `pr_title` references 12. Line 361, 362: `<pr-number>` references 13. Line 484: `pr_number` reference 14. Load that skill for the full rules on audience (AI agents), the cornerstone principle (cross-cutting insight), and what belongs in learned docs vs code comments. 20→ 21→## Agent Instructions 22→ 23→**Prerequisite:** Load the `learned-docs` skill for content quality standards before proceeding. 24→ 25→Tell the user (choose the appropriate pipeline display based on whether `.erk/impl-context/` exists): 26→ 27→**When `.erk/impl-context/` exists (CI/async mode):** 28→ 29→``` 30→Learn pipeline for PR #<pr-number> (using preprocessed materials):

720→``` 721→ 722→Parse the JSON output to get `pr_number`, `plan_backend`, `title`, and `pr_url` (the new learn plan number). 723→ 724→Display the result: 725→ 726→``` 727→Learn plan saved as draft PR #<pr_number>: <title> 728→URL: <pr_url> 729→``` 730→ 731→### Step 8: Store Tripwire Candidates on Learn Plan PR 732→ 733→**If plan was valid and saved**, store tripwire candidates as a metadata comment: 734→ 735→```bash 736→erk exec store-tripwire-candidates \ 737→ --issue <new-learn-plan-issue-number> \ 738→ --candidates-file .erk/scratch/sessions/${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}/learn-agents/tripwire-candidates.json 739→``` 740→ 741→This stores the tripwire candidates as a machine-readable metadata block comment on the learn plan, enabling `erk land` to read them directly without regex parsing. The store command automatically normalizes common schema drift (wrong root keys, field aliases, extra fields) before validation. 742→ 743→Parse the JSON output. If `count` is 0, no comment was added (no candidates found by the extractor). This is normal and not an error. 744→ 745→### Step 9: Track Learn Result on Parent Plan 746→ 747→**If plan was valid and saved**, update the parent plan's status to link the two issues: 748→ 749→```bash
... [5 lines omitted] ...

800→ 1. Submit for implementation (Recommended) — Queue for remote implementation 801→ 2. Review in browser — Open draft PR in web browser for review/editing 802→ 3. Done — Finish learn workflow 803→``` 804→ 805→**Execute the selected action:** 806→ 807→- **Submit**: Run `/erk:pr-dispatch` 808→- **Review**: Display the `pr_url` from the JSON output for the user to click. Then inform the user they can run `/erk:pr-dispatch` when ready. 809→- **Consolidate**: Run `/local:replan-learn-plans` 810→- **Done**: Proceed directly to Step 11 811→ 812→### Step 11: Track Evaluation 813→ 814→**CRITICAL: Always run this step**, regardless of which option was selected in Step 10. 815→ 816→This ensures `erk land` won't warn about unlearned plans: 817→ 818→```bash 819→erk exec track-learn-evaluation <issue-number> --session-id="${CLAUDE_SESSION_ID}"

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior. </system-reminder>

100→### Step 3: Get Session Information 101→ 102→Run the exec script to get session details: 103→ 104→```bash 105→erk exec get-learn-sessions <pr-number> 106→``` 107→ 108→Parse the JSON output to get: 109→ 110→- `session_sources`: List of session source objects, each containing: 111→ - `source_type`: Either "local" (from ~/.claude) or "remote" (from GitHub Actions) 112→ - `session_id`: The Claude Code session ID 113→ - `run_id`: GitHub Actions run ID (legacy, may be null) 114→ - `path`: File path to the session (for local sessions only) 115→ - `gist_url`: Raw gist URL for downloading session (for remote sessions) 116→- `planning_session_id`: Session ID that created the plan 117→- `implementation_session_ids`: Session IDs that executed the plan 118→- `local_session_ids`: Fallback sessions found locally (when no tracked sessions exist) 119→- `last_remote_impl_at`: Timestamp if implemented via GitHub Actions (remote) 120→- `last_remote_impl_run_id`: GitHub Actions run ID for remote implementation 121→- `last_remote_impl_session_id`: Claude Code session ID from remote implementation 122→ 123→If no sessions are found, inform the user and stop. 124→ 125→**Note on remote sessions:** Remote sessions appear in `session_sources` with `source_type: "remote"` and `path: null`. These sessions must be downloaded before processing (see Step 4). 126→ 127→Tell the user: 128→ 129→```

1→--- 2→name: existing-docs-checker 3→description: Search existing documentation to identify duplicates and contradictions before suggesting new docs 4→allowed-tools: 5→ - Read 6→ - Write 7→ - Glob 8→ - Grep 9→--- 10→ 11→# Existing Docs Checker Agent 12→ 13→Search `docs/learned/`, `.claude/commands/`, and `.claude/skills/` to identify what documentation already exists before suggesting duplicates during learn extraction. Also detect potential contradictions between existing docs and new insights. 14→ 15→## Input 16→ 17→You receive: 18→ 19→- `plan_title`: Title of the plan being analyzed (e.g., "Add parallel agent orchestration") 20→- `pr_title`: PR title if available (often similar to plan title) 21→- `search_hints`: Key terms extracted from plan title (e.g., "parallel", "agent", "orchestration") 22→ 23→## Search Process 24→ 25→1. **Generate search terms from inputs:** 26→ - Extract key nouns and concepts from plan_title 27→ - Combine with provided search_hints 28→ - Remove common words (the, a, an, to, for, with, etc.) 29→ 30→2. **Search documentation directories:**
... [55 lines omitted] ...
